common_name: White Ground Orchid (Pure-White Hardy Chinese Terrestrial Orchid)

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; 'Alba' is the white-flowered form of the Chinese ground orchid — the pure white flowers have a more refined, elegant quality than the typical magenta-pink of the species.

directions: Plant corms in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; 'Alba' is the pure-white flowered form of Bletilla striata (Chinese ground orchid) — one of the easiest and most rewarding terrestrial orchids for shade garden planting, producing clusters of small (1–1.5 inch), delicate, pure-white orchid-form flowers in April–May on upright stems above pleated, strap-shaped foliage. The flowers are genuinely orchid-shaped — the characteristic labellum (lip) and lateral petals are clearly visible in each small flower — providing the exotic quality of an orchid in a remarkably hardy, easy-to-grow terrestrial bulb that naturalizes reliably in zone 9 shade gardens. 'Alba' differs from the standard pink-magenta Bletilla striata in its pure-white flower color — a crisp, clean white with no pink tones that creates a refined, elegant effect in woodland shade plantings. The pleated, strap-shaped foliage (12–18 inches) is attractive through the growing season and the winter dormancy is unobtrusive. Ground orchids naturalize slowly over years, forming expanding clumps that can be divided in early spring before new growth begins. In zone 9, ground orchids grow best in the partial to full shade of deciduous trees where they receive some winter sun and full summer shade — the classic woodland edge condition of their natural habitat. Combine with hostas, ferns, and other shade perennials for spring bloom in the shadow garden.

common_name: Chinese Wild Ginger (Evergreen Silver-Mottled Shade Groundcover Ginger)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Asarum splendens spreads slowly by rhizomes to form a gradually expanding groundcover — plant 8–12 inches apart and allow 2–3 seasons for plants to fill in and create a solid mat.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Asarum splendens (Chinese wild ginger) is a superior species for Gulf South shade gardens because it is more heat-tolerant than the American native wild gingers (A. canadense, A. shuttleworthii) while providing the same excellent groundcover qualities — producing broad, heart-shaped, evergreen leaves with a dramatic silver-and-dark-green mottled pattern that creates one of the most ornamental groundcover displays available for full-shade positions. The silver mottling covers 50–70% of each leaf surface in irregular patterns, creating a luminous, almost metallic quality that reflects available light in dim shade positions and brightens dark garden areas that most groundcovers occupy without visual distinction. The genus name Asarum refers to the small, inconspicuous, brown-purple flowers produced at or below soil level in spring — they are hidden by the foliage but botanically characteristic. The ground-hugging habit (6–10 inches) and slow rhizomatous spread make Chinese wild ginger an outstanding groundcover for defined garden areas — it does not spread aggressively out of control but gradually fills a planting space over several seasons. More tolerant of zone 9 summer heat than American wild gingers, making it the preferred Asarum for Gulf South gardens. Outstanding under camellias, azaleas, and other shade-casting shrubs where it fills bare soil with ornamental year-round foliage.

common_name: Grapeleaf Anemone (Vigorous Pink Fall-Blooming Shade Tolerant Anemone)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Anemone tomentosa is more vigorous and spreading than Japanese anemone hybrids — plant where it has room to expand, or use physical root barriers to contain the spread.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Anemone tomentosa (Grapeleaf Anemone, sometimes listed as 'Robustissima') is a vigorous, spreading species of fall-blooming anemone from China — larger and more aggressive than the refined Japanese anemone hybrids but valued precisely for that vigor and excellent heat tolerance that makes it reliable at the warm edge of the Anemone range (zone 9). The large, lobed, grape-leaf-like foliage (giving the common name 'Grapeleaf Anemone') is bold and attractive in summer, followed by tall (3–4 foot) stalks of simple, cup-shaped, warm-pink flowers with bright yellow centers that bloom in August–October — providing valuable late-season color in shaded garden areas when most shade perennials have finished flowering. The fall bloom season and shade tolerance make Grapeleaf Anemone one of the relatively few plants that provide clear-colored flowers in partial shade through September and October in zone 9. The vigorous stoloniferous spread — more aggressive than Japanese anemone hybrids — is a practical consideration: plant in defined beds with physical edging or barriers, or in naturalistic settings where spreading is welcome. The spreading habit creates an effective groundcover under deciduous trees but can overtake smaller companions. More adaptable to clay soil than most anemones; tolerates periodic moisture variability better than the more refined hybrid types.

common_name: Barbara Karst Bougainvillea (Classic Magenta-Red Everblooming Tropical Vine)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average soil in full sun; bougainvilleas bloom most prolifically under slight water and fertilizer stress — avoid overwatering and excessive nitrogen which promote vegetative growth at the expense of flowering.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Barbara Karst is one of the most popular and widely planted bougainvillea cultivars for Gulf South gardens — a vigorous, large-scale vine producing brilliant magenta-red to cerise-red bracts (the showy colored structures surrounding the inconspicuous true flowers) through most of the year in frost-free conditions. In zone 9, Barbara Karst blooms most heavily in spring and fall when temperatures moderate; in winter (November–February) the vine may defoliate partially during cold spells and bloom is reduced. The brilliant magenta-red bract color is one of the most vivid in the warm-climate garden palette and creates an unmistakable tropical visual impact when the vine is in full bloom. Bougainvilleas bloom most prolifically under slight stress — dry-down periods between waterings, lean soil without excessive nitrogen, and root constriction (slightly root-bound in containers) all promote flower production over vegetative growth. This counterintuitive cultural requirement (bloom more when stressed) is the most important management principle for bougainvillea. The thorny stems require careful placement away from pedestrian areas. In zone 9, Barbara Karst may be killed to the ground by severe freezes but returns from the roots; cover with frost cloth during freezes below 28°F. Outstanding trained on walls, fences, and pergolas where the massive bloom display can be appreciated.

common_name: Sun Mandevilla (Tropical Vine with Large Showy Summer Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; provide a trellis, obelisk, or other support for the twining stems — mandevilla climbs by twining and requires vertical support to display its full ornamental potential.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Sun Mandevilla (Mandevilla x hybrida, marketed under trade names including 'Sun Parasol') is a tropical climbing vine producing large (3–5 inch diameter), trumpet-shaped flowers in shades of brilliant red, deep pink, rose, pale pink, or white throughout the warm season. The large, glossy, dark-green leaves and continuous production of oversized, flamboyant flowers make mandevilla one of the most dramatically ornamental vines for Gulf South container gardens, trellis plantings, and patio displays. In zone 9, mandevilla grows vigorously through the warm season (April–November), producing continuous flowers without deadheading. The twining stems require a trellis, post, or support structure to climb — without support, mandevilla sprawls as a mounding container plant, which is also ornamentally effective. Zone 9b and 10 gardeners can treat mandevilla as a perennial vine that returns from the roots after mild winters; in zone 9a, the plants may be killed by severe freezes but return from persistent roots with mulch protection, or be overwintered as container plants in a frost-free garage or greenhouse. Red-flowered cultivars are attractive to hummingbirds. Container-grown mandevilla requires more frequent watering and fertilization than in-ground plants — use a balanced liquid fertilizer every 2 weeks through the growing season for best bloom production.
